name: Vitamin D (Calciferols)
effects: Prevents vitamin D deficiency bone disease
contraindications: Avoid chronic high-dose unsupervised use because of hypercalcemia risk

sources.title: Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
Institute of Medicine vitamin D report (2011)

description: Vitamin D is produced in skin (primarily D3) and obtained from diet/supplements (D2 and D3), then converted to active metabolites. Core evidence supports deficiency prevention and bone-health roles, while many non-skeletal claims remain under investigation.
summary: Vitamin D (D2/D3) is an essential nutrient-prohormone for calcium homeostasis and bone health; deficiency and chronic excess are both clinically important.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: vitamin D increases intestinal calcium/phosphorus absorption and supports bone mineralization through vitamin D receptor signaling; broader immune/cardiometabolic effects remain tentative.
safetyNotes: Deficiency is associated with rickets and osteomalacia risk.
Chronic excessive intake can cause hypercalcemia with symptoms such as nausea, weakness, and kidney stone/renal complications.
Adult upper intake level context: about 4,000 IU/day unless medically supervised.
interactions: Can increase hypercalcemia risk when combined with thiazide diuretics.
Glucocorticoids can reduce vitamin D action and calcium-balance effects.
dosage: RDA framework: about 600–800 IU/day depending on age/life stage.
Higher supplemental intakes are sometimes used to reach serum 25(OH)D targets, but individualized monitoring is often needed.
Adult upper intake level context: about 4,000 IU/day without specialist supervision.
preparation: Available as vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) and vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol), including single-ingredient and calcium+vitamin D products.
regulatoryStatus: Essential nutrient with established dietary reference intakes; OTC supplement forms are common, and high-dose prescription calciferol regimens are used in deficiency care.
